content.full_text:
RAVI MEHTA
CHAIRMAN

FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION

December 4, 1995

Mr. Frank Zolin
Director
Department of Motor Vehicles
P.O. Box 932328
Sacramento, CA 94232-3280

Re: Your request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. I-95-383

Dear Mr. Zolin:

This is in response to your letter requesting advice
concerning the "revolving door" restrictions of the Political
Reform Act (the "Act")1 that will apply to you after you leave
your employment with the Department of Motor Vehicles. Since your
request is one of a general nature, we have provided an informal
response to your request.2

QUESTION

What restrictions does the Act impose upon your post-
government employment activities with respect to contacts with the
State of California?

1 Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations,
Sections 18000-18995. All references to regulations are to Title
2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

2 Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section
83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3).)

428 J STREET, SUITE 800, SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95814 (916) 322-5660

Our File No. I-95-383
Page 2


CONCLUSION

You will be subject to two types of restrictions:

o    For one year after you leave your employment with the
Department of Motor Vehicles, you may not represent any other
person for compensation before that agency.  This prohibition
would preclude you from making any formal or informal appearance,
or any oral or written communication, if the appearance or
communication is for the purpose of influencing the department's
administrative or legislative action.  You also may not represent
a person in any action or proceeding involving the issuance,
amendment, awarding, or revocation of a permit, license, grant, or
contract, or the sale or purchase of goods or property (referred
to hereinafter as "any specific action or proceeding").

The one-year ban on influencing your former agency applies to
the "state administrative agency" for which you worked, the
Department of Motor Vehicles.  Therefore, this ban does not
prohibit you from influencing administrative or legislative action
or any action or proceeding before other state agencies, the
Governor, or the Legislature.  However, "influencing" your former
agency includes contacts made with your former agency while
appearing before other state agencies, the Governor, or the
Legislature, if the appearance is made for the purpose of
influencing your former agency.

o    In addition, you are permanently prohibited from
representing another person or aiding, advising, counseling,
consulting or assisting in representing another person before any
court or state administrative agency (or officer or employee
thereof) in any judicial or other proceeding in which you
participated as a state administrative official.

The permanent ban prohibits your involvement in any
proceeding, or specific matter between two parties, in which you
participated while Director of the Department of Motor Vehicles.
You are prohibited from participating in such proceedings at the
Department of Motor Vehicles or any other state agency.

FACTS

You have been Director of the Department of Motor Vehicles.
You are resigning your position effective December 1, 1995.  You
seek general guidance concerning any restrictions impacting your
ability to make contacts with the State of California after you
leave your current employment.

Our File No. I-95-383
Page 3


ANALYSIS

An official who has left state service is subject to two
types of restrictions under the Act discussed below.3

One-Year Ban

The first type of restriction is a one-year prohibition on
making any appearance before your former agency for the purpose of
influencing administrative or legislative action or any specific
action or proceeding.

Section 87406(d)(1) of the Act provides that no officer or
designated employee of a state administrative agency:

" ... for a period of one year after leaving
office or employment, shall, for compensation, act
as agent or attorney for, or otherwise represent,
any other person, by making any formal or informal
appearance, or by making any oral or written
communication, before any state administrative
agency, or officer or employee thereof, for which
he or she worked or represented during the 12
months before leaving office or employment, if the
appearance or communication is made for the purpose
of influencing administrative or legislative
action, or influencing any action or proceeding
involving the issuance, amendment, awarding, or
revocation of a permit, license, grant, or
contract, or the sale or purchase of goods or
property. For purposes of this paragraph, an
appearance before a state administrative agency
does not include an appearance in a court of law,
before an administrative law judge, or before the
Worker's Compensation Appeals Board."

The Department of Motor Vehicles is a "state administrative
agency," and as Director of the Department of Motor Vehicles, you
would be considered an officer of that agency. (Section
87406(a).)

Pursuant to Section 87406, for one year after you leave the
Department of Motor Vehicles, you may not, for compensation, act

3 Please note that the Act also prohibits state administrative
officials from making, participating in making, or using their
official position to influence any governmental decision directly
relating to any person with whom the official is negotiating, or
has any arrangement concerning, prospective employment. (Section
87407.) Therefore, you would be precluded from making,
participating in making, or using your official position to
influence any governmental decision relating to a prospective
employer. (See, Symkowick Advice Letter, No. A-93-115.)

Our File No. I-95-383
Page 4


as representative or agent for any person before the Department⁴
for the purpose of influencing administrative⁵ or legislative⁶
action or any specific actions or proceedings. (Section 87406.)

You have not provided specific facts concerning your future
employment. Therefore, we are providing you the following general
guidance which may be of assistance to you.

Communications and Appearances: Under Section 87406, you
would be prohibited from making any communications or appearance to
influence any specific action or proceeding of the Department of
Motor Vehicles. Any communication such as telephone calls or
meetings with Department of Motor Vehicles staff that are intended
to influence administrative or legislative action or action on
such contracts, for example, are prohibited under Section 87406.
(Byrne Advice Letter, No. A-95-337.)

However, communications with the Department which are not for
the purpose of influencing administrative or legislative action
are not restricted by Section 87406. For example, you could
attend informational meetings with the Department of Motor
Vehicles, or request information from the Department concerning
existing laws, regulations, or policies, so long as you do not
attempt to influence administrative or legislative action. (See
Bagatelos Advice Letter, No. I-91-202; and Regulation
18202(a)(1).)

For the Purpose of Influencing: The one-year ban on
influencing your former agency only applies to the "state
administrative agency" for which you worked, the Department of
Motor Vehicles. Therefore, this ban does not prohibit you from
influencing administrative or legislative action or any specific

_________________________
4    You have not provided information concerning any boards or
commissions which may be considered part of your "state
administrative agency." For purposes of our analysis, it would
include entities which your agency controls. (Monagan Advice
Letter, No. A-93-473.)

5    "Administrative action" is defined in Section 82002 as the
proposal, drafting, development, consideration, amendment,
enactment or defeat by any state agency of any rule, regulation or
other action in any rate-making proceeding or any quasi-
legislative proceeding.

6    Section 82037 defines "legislative action" as the drafting,
introduction, consideration, modification, amendment or defeat of
any bill, resolution, amendment, report, nomination or other
matter by the Legislature or by either house or any committee,
subcommittee, joint or select committee thereof, or by a member or
employee of the Legislature acting in his or her official
capacity. "Legislative action" also means the action of the
Governor in approving or vetoing any bill.

Our File No. I-95-383
Page 5


action or proceeding. Thus, generally, you would not be
restricted by Section 87406 from lobbying the Legislature or
Governor regarding legislation. (See Witherspoon Advice Letter,
No. A-94-371; Craven Advice Letter, A-93-057.)

However, "influencing legislative or administrative action"
includes influencing by any means, including but not limited to
the provision or use of information, statistics, or analyses.
(Section 82032.) Therefore, "influencing legislative or
administrative action" includes contacts made with your former
agency while appearing before other state agencies, the Governor,
or the Legislature, if the appearance is made for the purpose of
influencing your former agency.

Employment Activities: The Act's revolving door restrictions
would not prohibit you from registering as a lobbyist or, as noted
above, from lobbying the Legislature or Governor. Moreover,
Section 87406 does not prohibit you from entering into consulting
agreements with private parties so long as in the conduct of your
duties you do not attempt to influence the Department of Motor
Vehicles' administrative or legislative action as discussed above,
or any specific action or proceeding before other state agencies,
the Governor, or the Legislature. (Byrne Advice Letter, supra.)

Permanent Prohibition

The second restriction is a permanent prohibition on
influencing any judicial or other proceeding in which the official
participated while in state service.

Sections 87401 and 87402 provide:

No former state administrative official, after
the termination of his or her employment or term of
office, shall for compensation act as agent or
attorney for, or otherwise represent, any other
person (other than the State of California) before
any court or state administrative agency or any
officer or employee thereof by making any formal or
informal appearance, or by making any oral or
written communication with the intent to influence,
in connection with any judicial, quasi-judicial or
other proceeding if both of the following apply:

(a) The State of California is a party or
has a direct and substantial interest.

(b) The proceeding is one in which the
former state administrative official
participated.

Section 87401.

Our File No. I-95-383
Page 6


No former state administrative official, after
the termination of his or her employment or term of
office shall for compensation aid, advise, counsel,
consult or assist in representing any other person
(except the state of California) in any proceeding
in which the official would be prohibited from
appearing under Section 87401.

Section 87402.

However, this restriction is limited to judicial, quasi-
judicial or other proceedings in which you participated. Section
87400 provides:

(c) "Judicial, quasi-judicial or other
proceeding" means any proceeding, application,
request for a ruling or other determination,
contract, claim, controversy, investigation,
charge, accusation, arrest or other particular
matter involving a specific party or parties in any
court or state administrative agency, including but
not limited to any proceeding governed by Chapter 5
(commencing with Section 11500) of Division 3 of
Title 2 of the Government Code.

(d) "Participated" means to have taken part
personally and substantially through decision,
approval, disapproval, formal written
recommendation, rendering advice on a substantial
basis, investigation or use of confidential
information as an officer or employee, but
excluding approval, disapproval or rendering of
legal advisory opinions to departmental or agency
staff which do not involve a specific party or
parties.

Proceedings Before Any Court or Other State Administrative
Agency: The permanent ban of Sections 87401 and 87402 applies
only to judicial, quasi-judicial or other proceedings before any
court or state administrative agency in which you participated
while at the Department of Motor Vehicles. Section 87400(a),
however, expressly defines "state administrative agency" to
exclude the Legislature.

Participation: As Director of the Department of Motor
Vehicles, you are deemed to have "participated" in any proceeding
which you supervised or which was handled by the Department of
Motor Vehicles staff during your tenure. (Byrne Advice Letter,
supra; Brown Advice Letter, No. A-91-033.)

Proceeding Involving Specific Parties: Under Section
87400(c) set forth above, a "proceeding" includes any particular
matter involving a specific party or parties in a state
administrative agency. For example, a lawsuit would be considered

Our File No. I-95-383
Page 7


a judicial, quasi-judicial or other proceeding involving specific
parties. (Witherspoon Advice Letter, supra.) In addition, any
contractual arrangements between the Department of Motor Vehicles
and a private party would constitute a proceeding in which you
participated at the Department. You would be permanently
prohibited from representing the private party, or assisting in
representing that party, before any state administrative agency
regarding the contracts.

We hope this is helpful to you in providing an overview of
the "revolving door" provisions. If you have any further
questions regarding this matter, or seek further advice on a
specific aspect of these laws, please feel free to contact me at
(916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Steven G. Churchwell
General Counsel

By: Luisa Menchaca
Counsel, Legal Division
